title: Incident Playbook: When a Domain Hits Spam

questions.question: What are the four causes of a sudden inbox placement drop?

options.text: Bad internet, server outage, tool malfunction, billing issue
Spam trap hit, complaint spike, content filter trigger, IP/domain reputation drop
Gmail update, Outlook update, Yahoo update, DNS expiration
Sending too few emails, not enough warmup, wrong time zone, holiday period

questions.explanation: The four causes are: spam trap hit (sending to recycled or pristine trap addresses), complaint spike (multiple spam reports in a short window), content filter trigger (matching spam patterns), and IP/domain reputation drop (accumulated negative signals crossing a threshold).

questions.question: What is a spam trap and why is it dangerous?

options.text: A tool that filters spam from your inbox
An email address that exists only to catch spammers — no real person uses it, so sending to one proves you're not validating your list
A feature in Gmail that catches suspicious emails

questions.explanation: Spam traps are email addresses that exist only to catch spammers. They come in two types: pristine (never used by a real person) and recycled (abandoned for 12+ months then repurposed). Sending to one proves to ISPs that you're not properly validating your email list, triggering immediate reputation penalties.

questions.question: What separates professionals from amateurs when it comes to spam incidents?

options.text: Professionals detect incidents within 24 hours and execute a recovery protocol; amateurs don't know they're in spam for weeks

questions.explanation: Every sender hits spam eventually. The difference is detection speed (within 24 hours vs weeks of unknowing) and having a recovery protocol. Professionals monitor placement daily, detect drops immediately, and execute specific recovery steps that can resolve issues in 48 hours vs losing domains permanently.

questions.question: Every sender hits spam eventually — the difference between success and failure is having a detection and recovery system in place.
questions.explanation: True. Spam incidents are inevitable regardless of how careful you are. The lesson emphasizes that incident response capability — fast detection, accurate diagnosis, and proven recovery protocols — is what separates professionals from amateurs.

questions.question: Design your spam incident response plan. What monitoring would you check first if you suspected a deliverability problem? What are the first three actions you would take within 60 minutes of detecting a drop in inbox placement?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response includes specific monitoring tools (Google Postmaster, Instantly health check, SNDS), describes a logical diagnostic sequence (check authentication, review recent sends, check complaint rates), and lists concrete first-60-minute actions (pause sends, check spam trap databases, review content). Look for a structured, actionable plan.
